items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Estimating shark attacks as common because the news is vivid is:
items.why: It's Availability heuristic. “Anchoring” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Fast vs slow thinking. “STM vs LTM” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Kahneman published 'Thinking, Fast and Slow':
items.why: Exact answer: 2011. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
